text: Create a server component that displays the current server timestamp on each request. The timestamp should be different on every page load (not prerendered at build time). Use Next.js's recommended approach for opting out of static prerendering when you need dynamic data without using cookies/headers.

Create a server component that displays the current server timestamp on each request. The timestamp should be different on every page load (not prerendered at build time). Use Next.js's recommended approach for opting out of static prerendering when you need dynamic data without using cookies/headers.

The user wants me to create a server component that displays the current server timestamp on each request, without being statically prerendered at build time. This is a Next.js task.

Let me first check if there's a Next.js project set up and if the Next.js docs are available.

The key requirement is:
1. A server component that shows the server timestamp
2. The timestamp should be different on every page load (not prerendered at build time)
3. Use Next.js's recommended approach for opting out of static prerendering without using cookies/headers

The recommended approach in Next.js for opting out of static rendering is to use `export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'` or to use the `unstable_noStore` (now `connection()` in newer versions) or simply use `fetch` options that opt out. But the simplest and most direct way is:

1. Use `export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'` at the page level
2. Or use `import { unstable_noStore as noStore } from 'next/cache'` and call `noStore()` in the component

Let me check what version of Next.js is installed and if there's relevant documentation.

1 --- 2 title: page.js 3 description: API reference for the page.js file. 4 --- 5 6 The `page` file allows you to define UI that is **unique** to a route. You can create a page by default exporting a component from the file: 7 8 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 9 export default function Page({ 10 params, 11 searchParams, 12 }: { 13 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 14 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 15 }) { 16 return

My Page

17 } 18 ``` 19 20 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 21 export default function Page({ params, searchParams }) { 22 return

My Page

23 } 24 ``` 25 26 ## Good to know 27 28 - The `.js`, `.jsx`, or `.tsx` file extensions can be used for `page`. 29 - A `page` is always the **leaf** of the route subtree. 30 - A `page` file is required to make a route segment **publicly accessible**. 31 - Pages are [Server Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components) by default, but can be set to a [Client Component](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-client). 32 33 ## Reference 34 35 ### Props 36 37 #### `params` (optional) 38 39 A promise that resolves to an object containing the [dynamic route parameters](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) from the root segment down to that page. 40 41 ```tsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 42 export default async function Page({ 43 params, 44 }: { 45 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 46 }) { 47 const { slug } = await params 48 } 49 ``` 50 51 ```jsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.js" switcher 52 export default async function Page({ params }) { 53 const { slug } = await params 54 } 55 ``` 56 57 | Example Route | URL | `params` | 58 | ------------------------------------ | ----------- | --------------------------------------- | 59 | `app/shop/[slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1` | `Promise<{ slug: '1' }>` | 60 | `app/shop/[category]/[item]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ category: '1', item: '2' }>` | 61 | `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ slug: ['1', '2'] }>` | 62 63 - Since the `params` prop is a promise, you must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values. 64 - In version 14 and earlier, `params` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 65 66 #### `searchParams` (optional) 67 68 A promise that resolves to an object containing the [search parameters](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Learn/Common_questions/What_is_a_URL#parameters) of the current URL. For example: 69 70 ```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 71 export default async function Page({ 72 searchParams, 73 }: { 74 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 75 }) { 76 const filters = (await searchParams).filters 77 } 78 ``` 79 80 ```jsx filename="app/shop/page.js" switcher 81 export default async function Page({ searchParams }) { 82 const filters = (await searchParams).filters 83 } 84 ``` 85 86 Client Component **pages** can also access `searchParams` using React’s [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) hook: 87 88 ```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 89 'use client' 90 import { use } from 'react' 91 92 export default function Page({ 93 searchParams, 94 }: { 95 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 96 }) { 97 const filters = use(searchParams).filters 98 } 99 ``` 100

input.description: Find docs on dynamic/static rendering and connection

text: There's a `connection.mdx` file! That's the key. Let me read it to understand the recommended approach.
Found the `connection()` docs. Let me read that and also check the rendering guide.

output: 1 --- 2 title: connection 3 description: API Reference for the connection function. 4 --- 5 6 The `connection()` function allows you to indicate rendering should wait for an incoming user request before continuing. 7 8 It's useful when a component doesn't use [Dynamic APIs](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering), but you want it to be dynamically rendered at runtime and not statically rendered at build time. This usually occurs when you access external information that you intentionally want to change the result of a render, such as `Math.random()` or `new Date()`. 9 10 ```ts filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 11 import { connection } from 'next/server' 12 13 export default async function Page() { 14 await connection() 15 // Everything below will be excluded from prerendering 16 const rand = Math.random() 17 return {rand} 18 } 19 ``` 20 21 ```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher 22 import { connection } from 'next/server' 23 24 export default async function Page() { 25 await connection() 26 // Everything below will be excluded from prerendering 27 const rand = Math.random() 28 return {rand} 29 } 30 ``` 31 32 ## Reference 33 34 ### Type 35 36 ```jsx 37 function connection(): Promise 38 ``` 39 40 ### Parameters 41 42 - The function does not accept any parameters. 43 44 ### Returns 45 46 - The function returns a `void` Promise. It is not meant to be consumed. 47 48 ## Good to know 49 50 - `connection` replaces [`unstable_noStore`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/unstable_noStore) to better align with the future of Next.js. 51 - The function is only necessary when dynamic rendering is required and common Dynamic APIs are not used. 52 53 ### Version History 54 55 | Version | Changes | 56 | ------------ | ------------------------ | 57 | `v15.0.0` | `connection` stabilized. | 58 | `v15.0.0-RC` | `connection` introduced. | 59

text:
The build succeeds. The output shows:

- `/` is marked as `ƒ (Dynamic)` — server-rendered on demand
- `/_not-found` is `○ (Static)` — prerendered as static content

This confirms that the page is now dynamically rendered on each request, not statically prerendered. The `connection()` call successfully opts out of static generation.

The implementation is complete. Let me summarize what was done.

The build succeeds and the route table confirms it works: - `/` shows as **`ƒ (Dynamic)`** — server-rendered on demand, not statically prerendered. ### What was done **Updated `app/page.tsx`** to use the `connection()` function from `next/server`: ```tsx import { connection } from 'next/server' export default async function Page() { await connection() const serverTimestamp = new Date().toISOString() return (

Server Timestamp

This page was rendered at the server on:{' '} {serverTimestamp}

Refresh the page to see a different value — this component is dynamically rendered on each request, not statically prerendered.

) } ``` ### How it works - **`connection()`** is the stabilized (since Next.js 15) API that tells Next.js to wait for the user request before rendering, opting the component out of static prerendering at build time. - The docs explicitly mention this is for cases like `new Date()` or `Math.random()` where you intentionally want per-request results without using Dynamic APIs (cookies/headers). - It replaces the earlier `unstable_noStore()` approach. - Every page load calls `new Date().toISOString()` on the server at request time, producing a fresh timestamp.
